content: The molar conductances of $\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2, \mathrm{BaCl}_2$ and $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}$ at infinite dilution are $523 \cdot 28,280 \cdot 0$ and $129.8 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. The molar conductance of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ at infinite dilution will be
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { The equation for molar conductance is }\\ &\begin{aligned} 2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}\right)}^{\infty} & =2 \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}\right)}^{\infty}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{BaCl}_2\right)}^{\infty}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2\right)}^{\infty} \\ & =2 \times(129.8)-280+523.28=\frac{502.88}{2} \\ \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}\right)}^{\infty} & =251.44 \mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$\mathrm{BrF}_3$$ self ionises as $$\mathrm{BrF}_2^{+}$$ and $$\mathrm{BrF}_4^{-}$$.

$$2 \mathrm{BrF}_3 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{BrF}_2^{+}+\mathrm{BrF}_4^{-}$$.

It is because of their relatively more stable structure.

content: The equivalent conductance of NaCl, HCl and CH$$_3$$COONa at infinite dilution are 126.45, 426.16 and 91 ohm$$^{-1}$$cm$$^2$$eq$$^{-1}$$ respectively at 25$$^\circ$$C. The equivalent conductance of acetic acid (at infinite dilution) would be

options.content: cannot be determined from the given data.

content: Which of the following solutions will have highest conductivity?
explanation: Among the given solutions, NaCl, KNO<sub>3</sub> and HCl are strong electrolytes but conductivity also depends upon size of ions. Smaller the size of ions, greater is conductance, hence greater is the conductivity than larger ions. So, out of NaCl, KNO<sub>3</sub> and HCl ions, H<sup>+</sup> is the smallest and therefore, 0.1 M HCl will have highest conductivity.

content: The reduction potential of hydrogen half-cell will be negative if
explanation:
Reduction hydrogen half-cell is H+|(xM)|Pt(H2); Pressure pH2

Half-cell reaction is

2H+ (aq) + 2e$$-$$ $$\to$$ H2(g)

n = 2

Reaction quotient $$ \Rightarrow Q = {{{p_{{H_2}}}} \over {{{[{H^ + }]}^2}}}$$

$${E_{red}} = E_{red}^o - {{0.0591} \over n}\log Q$$

$${E_{red}} = - {{0.0591} \over 2}\log Q$$

Case (a), $$\log Q = \log {1 \over 1} = 0$$ $$\therefore$$ Ered = 0

Case (b), $$\log Q = \log {1 \over 4} = - ve$$ $$\therefore$$ Ered = +ve

Case (c), $$\log Q = \log {2 \over 1} = + ve$$ $$\therefore$$ Ered = $$-$$ve

Case (d), $$\log Q = \log {2 \over 4} = - ve$$ $$\therefore$$ Ered = +ve

$$\therefore$$ Ered is negative for option (c).

content: The charge carried by 1 millimole of M<sup>n+</sup> ions is 193 coulombs. The value of n is
explanation:
One millimole = 0.001 mol

i.e. 1 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$3 mol.

$$\because$$ 96500 C is required for 1 mol of electrons.

$$\therefore$$ 193 C give $${{193 \times 1} \over {96500}}$$ = 0.002 mol, i.e. 2 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$3 mol of electrons.

Thus, value of n in Mn+ is = $${{0.002} \over {0.001}} = 2$$

Hence, option (b) is the correct answer.

content: If electrolysis of aqueous CuSO<sub>4</sub> solution is carried out using Cu-electrodes, the reaction taking place at the anode is
explanation:
Electrolysis of copper sulphate solution using Cu-electrodes,

Ions present : Cu2+ , H2+, SO$$_4^{2 - }$$, OH$$-$$

At cathode : Cu2+(aq) + 2e$$-$$ $$\to$$ Cu(s)

At anode : Cu(s) $$-$$ 2e$$-$$ $$\to$$ Cu2+ (aq)

So, the option (d) is correct answer.

content: What amount of electricity can deposit 1 mole of Al metal at cathode when passed through molten AlCl<sub>3</sub> ?
explanation:
The number of electrons involved in the reaction are three as shown below

Al3+ + 3e$$-$$ $$\to$$ Al

It means the conversion of every aluminium ion to aluminium atom requires three electrons.

Therefore, the amount of electricity required for one mole of Al3+ ions = 3F.

Given,

$${Zn \to Z{n^{2 + }} + 2{e^ - };}$$ $${{E^o} = + 0.76V}$$ ..... (i)

$${Fe \to F{e^{2 + }} + 2{e^ - };}$$ $${{E^o} = + 0.41V}$$ ..... (ii)

On reversing the above reaction (i) and (ii), we get

$${Z{n^{2 + }} + 2{e^ - } \to Zn;}$$ $${{E^o} = - 0.76V}$$

$${F{e^{2 + }} + 2{e^ - } \to Fe;}$$ $${{E^o} = - 0.41V}$$

[where, E$$^\circ$$ = standard reduction potential]

To find, the standard emf of the cell with the reaction.


So, $$E_{cell}^o = E_{F{e^{2 + }}/Fe}^o - E_{Z{n^{2 + }}/Zn}^o$$

= $$-$$0.41 V + 0.76 V = + 0.35 V

content: How many faradays are required to reduce 1 mol of $$C{r_2}O_7^{2 - }$$ to Cr<sup>3+</sup> in acid medium?
explanation:
$$C{r_2}O_7^{2 - }$$ $$\to $$ Cr3+ change in +3 Oxi no. = +6

Total change in number of electrons = 2 $$\times$$ 3 = 6 mole = 6F

content: The order of equivalent conductance at infinite dilution for LiCl, NaCl and KCl is
explanation:
$$\because$$ KCl is more ionic than NaCl while NaCl is more ionic than LiCl.

Also, more the ionic nature, more is the conductivity thus, order of equivalent conductance at infinite dilution is KCl > NaCl > LiCl.

options.content: The oxidation number of $$\mathrm{Cr}$$ in $$\mathrm{CrO}_5$$ is +6
$$\Delta \mathrm{H}>\Delta \mathrm{U}$$ for the reaction $$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_2$$ (g), provided both gases behave ideally
$$\mathrm{pH}$$ of $$0.1(\mathrm{~N}) \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$$ is less than that of $$0.1(\mathrm{~N}) \mathrm{HCl}$$ at $$25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$

explanation:
Let's analyze each of the given statements one by one:

Option A: The oxidation number of $$\mathrm{Cr}$$ in $$\mathrm{CrO}_5$$ is +6.

To determine the oxidation state of $$\mathrm{Cr}$$ in $$\mathrm{CrO}_5$$, we need to know the structure of $$\mathrm{CrO}_5$$. The compound $$\mathrm{CrO}_5$$ has a peroxide linkage.

Let's calculate the oxidation state:

  • The oxidation state of $$\mathrm{O}$$ in peroxides ($$\mathrm{O}_2^{2-}$$) is -1.
  • For the other two oxygens (not in peroxides), the oxidation state is -2 each.

Let the oxidation state of $$\mathrm{Cr}$$ in $$\mathrm{CrO}_5$$ be $$x$$:

$$x + 2(-1) + 2(-2) = 0$$

Solving for $$x$$:

$$x - 2 - 4 = 0$$

$$x = 6$$

Thus, the oxidation state of $$\mathrm{Cr}$$ is indeed +6. Option A is correct.

Option B: $$\Delta \mathrm{H}>\Delta \mathrm{U}$$ for the reaction $$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_2$$ (g), provided both gases behave ideally.

We use the relation:

$$\Delta \mathrm{H} = \Delta \mathrm{U} + \Delta n \mathrm{RT}$$

Here, $$\Delta n$$ is the change in the number of moles of gases. For the reaction:

$$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$$

$$\Delta n = 2 - 1 = 1$$. Since $$\Delta n$$ is positive, we have:

$$\Delta \mathrm{H} = \Delta \mathrm{U} + \Delta n \mathrm{RT}$$

This implies:

$$\Delta \mathrm{H} > \Delta \mathrm{U}$$

Hence, Option B is correct.

Option C: $$\mathrm{pH}$$ of $$0.1(\mathrm{~N}) \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$$ is less than that of $$0.1(\mathrm{~N}) \mathrm{HCl}$$ at $$25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$.

Option (C) is incorrect because $0.1(\mathrm{~N}) \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ furnishes less $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion than $0.1(\mathrm{~N}) \mathrm{HCl}$, So, $\mathrm{pH}$ of the former solution will be more than the later.

Option D: $$\left(\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{F}}\right)=0.0591$$ volt at $$25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$.

Let's calculate $$\left(\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{F}}\right)$$ at $$25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$:

  • $$\mathrm{R} = 8.314$$ J/mol·K
  • $$\mathrm{T} = 298$$ K (25°C)
  • $$\mathrm{F} = 96485$$ C/mol

So:

$$\left(\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{F}}\right) = \frac{8.314 \times 298}{96485}$$

On calculation:

$$\left(\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{F}}\right) \approx 0.0257$$ V

This value is not 0.0591 V. Thus, Option D is incorrect.

Summarizing, the correct statements are Option A and Option B.

options.content: The oxidation number of Cr in CrO<sub>5</sub> is + 6.
$$\Delta$$H > $$\Delta$$U for the reaction N<sub>2</sub>O<sub>4</sub>(g) $$\to$$ 2NO<sub>2</sub>(g) provided both gases behave ideally.
pH of 0.1 N H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub> is less than that of 0.1 N HCl at 25$$^\circ$$C.

explanation:
(a) In CrO5 oxidation no. of Cr is + 6.


(b) For the reaction (in ideal case)

N2O4(g) $$\to$$ 2NO2(g)

$$\Delta$$H = $$\Delta$$U + p . $$\Delta$$V

$$\because$$ $$\Delta$$H = $$\Delta$$U + $$\Delta$$ng RT

($$\because$$ p$$\Delta$$V = $$\Delta$$ngRT)

where, $$\Delta$$ng = (gaseous moles of product) $$-$$ (gaseous moles of reactant)

$$\Delta$$ng = 2 $$-$$ 1 = 1

Thus, $$\Delta$$H > $$\Delta$$U

(c) pH of 0.1 N H2SO4 is less than of 0.1 HCl at 25$$^\circ$$ is a wrong statement.

pH of H2SO4

$$\because$$ pH = $$-$$log[H+] = $$-$$ log[10$$-$$2]

$$\because$$ (N = C $$\times$$ Z) and (Z = 2 for H2SO4)

pH = 2 and pH of HCl

pH = $$-$$ log[10$$-$$1= - 1

Thus, pH of H2SO4 > pH of HCl.

(d) $${{{RT} \over F}}$$ = 0.0591 at 25$$^\circ$$C is a wrong statement.

Where, R = Gas constant = 8.314

T = Temperature = 273 + 25 = 298 K

F = Charge over one mole of electrons = 96500 C

Thus, $${{RT} \over F} = {{8.314 \times 298} \over {96500}} = 0.02567$$

The correct value is $${{2.303\,RT} \over F} = 0.0591$$

Hence, only option (a) and (b) are correct.

content: The role of fluorspar, which is added in small quantities in the electrolytic reduction of alumina dissolved in fused cryolite is

options.content: to lower the melting temperature of the mixture
to decrease the rate of oxidation of carbon at anode

explanation: Among the given statements (b) and (c) are correct these are the facts.
